title: Dysphagia & Aspiration Precautions in an Older Adult with Parkinson's Disease
subtitle: A 79-year-old man with Parkinson's disease was admitted with aspiration pneumonia and is now improving. The nurse must recognize dysphagia, keep him safe until a swallow evaluation, and apply correct aspiration precautions during feeding.

patient.name: Walter Briggs
patient.admitDate: Admitted 3 days ago with aspiration pneumonia. Parkinson's disease for 8 years. Now afebrile-trending and improving on IV antibiotics. Reports food 'sticking' and coughs with thin liquids. Awaiting swallow evaluation before resuming oral intake.
patient.room: Med-surg; HOB elevated, on 2 L nasal cannula, IV antibiotics

vitals.time: Today — trial sips of thin water
vitals.spo2: dipped to 90% with wet, gurgly voice and coughing

vitals.temp: Pain 2/10; alert, no distress at rest

labs.v: FAILED — cough and wet voice with thin liquids.
labs.r: Keep NPO; obtain formal SLP evaluation.

labs.v: Pharyngeal-phase delay; silent aspiration of thin liquids.

nursesNote: Walter Briggs, 79, Parkinson's disease, day 3 for aspiration pneumonia and improving. Coughs and has a wet, gurgly voice with thin liquids; reports food sticking; chews slowly and pockets food. Bedside swallow screen failed. Currently NPO pending SLP evaluation. HOB up, suction at bedside.
providerOrders: Consult Speech-Language Pathology for swallow evaluation
Aspiration precautions; HOB elevated; suction available at bedside
Continue IV antibiotics and IV fluids
Advance diet per SLP recommendations; oral care every shift

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What signals dysphagia and aspiration risk?
steps.inst: Select ALL items that suggest dysphagia or aspiration risk.

opts.text: Coughing or throat-clearing during/after swallowing thin liquids
Wet, 'gurgly' voice quality after swallowing
Pocketing food in the cheeks; slow, effortful chewing
opts.rat: Impaired oral phase, common in Parkinson's.

opts.text: A present gag reflex confirms the swallow is safe
opts.rat: Gag reflex does NOT predict swallow safety.

opts.text: Drooling and trouble managing oral secretions
Multiple swallows per bite and very long mealtimes
Parkinson's disease impairs the oral and pharyngeal swallow phases
opts.rat: A leading neurologic cause of dysphagia.

opts.text: Silent aspiration can occur with NO cough; clear lungs don't rule it out
opts.rat: Absence of coughing is not reassuring.

opts.text: The right-lower-lobe infiltrate fits an aspiration event
opts.rat: Aspirate commonly tracks to the RLL.

opts.text: Dysphagia is a normal part of aging and needs no workup
opts.rat: Dysphagia is never 'normal aging' — evaluate it.

opts.text: Low albumin and high BUN point to poor intake / dehydration risk
opts.rat: Nutrition and hydration are at risk.

opts.text: A formal swallow evaluation is needed before oral intake resumes
Keep him NPO until a swallow screen/evaluation is completed
Position fully upright (90°) and ensure suction is at the bedside
Notify the provider and request an SLP swallow evaluation
Provide oral care to reduce oral bacterial load
Plan diet texture and feeding teaching per SLP results

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Classify each aspiration-precaution measure as Recommended or Avoid.

opts.text: Sit fully upright (90°) for meals and 30–60 min after
Thickened liquids and modified textures as the SLP recommends
Small bites, slow pace, chin-tuck, single sips
Alternate solids and liquids; check the mouth for pocketing
Oral care before and after every meal
Minimize distractions; no talking with food in the mouth
Feed him while reclined, drowsy, or lying flat
Give thin water or juice when thickened liquids are ordered
Rush feeding with large or stacked spoonfuls
Lay him flat immediately after eating

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: SLP cleared a nectar-thick, mechanical-soft diet — sequence the feeding
steps.inst: Rank the order of safe-feeding actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm he is awake/alert and verify the current diet order
opts.rat: #1 — Never feed a drowsy patient or wrong texture.

opts.text: Position him fully upright at 90° with suction ready
Offer small bites at a slow pace using a chin-tuck
Check the mouth for pocketing before the next bite
Provide oral care and keep him upright 30–60 min after
The UAP kept him upright for 45 minutes after lunch
The nurse stopped feeding and reported a new wet, gurgly voice
opts.rat: Recognized and escalated a danger sign.

opts.text: Liquids were thickened to nectar consistency as ordered
Feeding was slow with rest breaks and pocketing checks
Oral care was given after the meal
The UAP laid him flat right after eating
Family gave sips of plain water 'because he was thirsty'
The tray was left for him to self-feed while drowsy, unsupervised
Coughing during sips was ignored and feeding continued
